plainMeaning: The community is like a great mountain whose waterfalls of self-restraint shine like a necklace, and whose caves echo with crowds of lay-devotees and dancing peacocks.
recitation: saṁvara vara jala pagaliya, ujjhara pavirāyamāṇa hārassa. sāvaga jaṇa paüra ravanta, mora naccanta kuharassa.

wordByWord.gloss: self-restraint; the stopping of karmic inflow
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṁvara, from sam + √vṛ 'to cover, restrain'; the dam that halts āsrava (karmic influx).
wordByWord.notes: Here imaged as the pure water that feeds the mountain's cascades.
The 'water of saṁvara' that streams down the slopes.

wordByWord.etymology: Past participle, Pkt. < Skt. pragalita, from pra + √gal 'to flow, ooze, drip'.
Pkt. ujjhara / nijjhara < Skt. nirjhara 'a fall of water from a height'.
wordByWord.notes: The streams that, gleaming, form the mountain's ornament.

wordByWord.etymology: Present participle, Pkt. < Skt. pra-virājamāna, from pra + vi + √rāj 'to shine forth, be resplendent'.
wordByWord.notes: The cascade glitters like jewels strung on a thread.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive sg. of hāra 'pearl-necklace, garland', from √hṛ 'to carry, wear'; Pkt. gen. ending -ssa < Skt. -sya.
wordByWord.notes: Bahuvrīhi-genitive: the gleaming cascade is the mountain's hāra. The -ssa continues the long genitive chain describing Meru that runs across the surrounding verses (cf. guhassa before, vaṇassa after).

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. sāvaga < Skt. śrāvaka 'hearer, lay follower', from √śru 'to hear'.
wordByWord.notes: Two of the four limbs of the Saṅgha are evoked through the laity here.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. paüra < Skt. pracura 'much, abundant, numerous'.
Present participle, Pkt. < Skt. ravat, from √ru 'to roar, cry, sound'.
wordByWord.notes: The hum of devotional voices fills the caverns.
An auspicious creature whose dance betokens natural delight.

wordByWord.etymology: Present participle, Pkt. naccanta < Skt. nṛtyat, from √nṛt 'to dance'.
wordByWord.notes: Modifies mora — peacocks dancing in the glens.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive sg. of kuhara 'cave, hollow, glen', from √kuh; Pkt. gen. -ssa < Skt. -sya.
wordByWord.notes: Second genitive epithet of the verse, paralleling hārassa: 'whose caves resound with lay-folk and dancing peacocks'.

literalTranslation: Of [that great mountain] whose gleaming necklace is its cascades, trickling down with the choicest water of self-restraint; whose caves resound abundantly with throngs of lay-devotees and with dancing peacocks.
idiomaticTranslation: Behold the Saṅgha as the world-mountain: its slopes are laced with shining waterfalls — the pure, streaming water of self-restraint — strung like a glittering necklace across its flanks; and its caverns ring out, thronged with crowds of devoted laypeople and loud with the cry of dancing peacocks.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā carries forward the sustained mountain-portrait, adding two fresh ornaments to the towering Saṅgha-Meru: its waterfalls and its caves. Where the previous verse left the mountain's flanks and herb-bright caverns, this verse animates them. The cascades (ujjhara) are no ordinary mountain streams — their water is saṁvara, self-restraint itself, 'choicest' (vara) because it is the very water that stops the inflow of karma. Tumbling and glittering down the slopes (pagaliya … pavirāyamāṇa), these bright streams hang upon the mountain like a pearl necklace (hāra) — a single, precisely-built image: restraint, lived openly, adorns the community as visibly and beautifully as a jeweled garland adorns a peak. The second half turns from sight to sound. The mountain's caves (kuhara) are not silent and empty but paüra — densely thronged — ringing (ravanta) with crowds of lay-devotees (sāvaga jaṇa) and loud with peacocks dancing (mora naccanta) in delight. The poet thus weaves the human and the natural into one auspicious clamor: the laity gathered in glad reverence, and the peacock — emblem of rain-fed joy — dancing as if at a festival. Note the grammar that holds the verse together: both halves end in a genitive (hārassa, kuharassa), each a bahuvrīhi epithet of the unnamed mountain, links in the long descriptive chain that threads through the neighbouring verses. The verse needs no main clause of its own; it simply heaps two more glories onto Meru, and through Meru onto the Saṅgha — restraint as its shining adornment, and the joyous press of devotees as its living voice.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse turns on saṁvara — in Jain doctrine the stoppage of karmic influx (āsrava-nirodhaḥ saṁvaraḥ, Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.1). By making saṁvara the 'water' of the cascades, the poet reverses the ordinary expectation that water erodes: here restraint is the flowing, life-giving, beautifying force of the spiritual mountain, and its visible practice is the community's most splendid ornament. The thronging śrāvakas in the caves quietly affirm the fourfold Saṅgha: the laity are not peripheral to the mountain's grandeur but fill its very recesses with sound and life. The dancing peacock adds the note that a life of restraint is not grim but festal — natural creation itself rejoices around the abode of discipline.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter is invited to see their own self-restraint not as a dull privation but as something luminous — a necklace one wears openly, beautifying the whole community. The verse also dignifies the lay practitioner: like the śrāvaka-throngs filling Meru's caves, the ordinary devotee's glad presence is itself part of the Saṅgha's splendour. One chants it to reframe discipline as adornment and gathering as joy.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.1: āsrava-nirodhaḥ saṁvaraḥ ('the stoppage of influx is saṁvara')

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Root-forms and meanings of nirjhara (ujjhara), pragalita (pagaliya), pra-virājamāna (pavirāyamāṇa), pracura (paüra), kuhara, mayūra (mora), and √nṛt (naccanta)

sources.title: Jain eLibrary (jainqq.org) — Ardhamagadhi/Prakrit lexical resources
sources.consultedFor: Ardhamagadhi/Prakrit glosses for sāvaga (śrāvaka), saṁvara, and genitive -ssa morphology

sources.title: Nandī-sūtra (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Nandī-sūtra and its opening Saṅgha-praise within which this Meru simile sits

notes: The two -ssa forms (hārassa, kuharassa) are genitive singulars functioning as bahuvrīhi epithets of an unnamed mountain (Meru/Mandara) whose description spans several adjacent verses (cf. guhassa in the preceding verse, vaṇassa in the following); this verse therefore has no independent main clause. 'saṁvara vara jala' is read as 'the choicest water [which is] saṁvara'; an alternative parsing 'the excellent water OF saṁvara' yields the same devotional sense. 'ujjhara pavirāyamāṇa hāra' is read as 'the gleaming cascade [as the mountain's] necklace'. Spellings (ઉજ્ઝર, પગલિય, પવિરાયમાણ, પઉર) follow the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book verbatim and are not normalized. Recensions of the Nandī opening vary slightly in wording; exact mātrās should be confirmed against a critical edition where precise reading matters.
